The Six Pillar Framework (P1-P6): - **P1: Product & Distribution (Design)**: Focus on New Product features, Target Market, and Benefit Design. [Linked Themes: Product Design, Group Products, CI, IP, LTC, PMI, State Healthcare].
- **P2: Pricing & Underwriting (Sell)**: Focus on Assumptions (Morbidity/Mortality), Rating Factors, and Selective Underwriting. [Linked Themes: Pricing, Underwriting].
- **P3: Claims Management (Pay)**: Focus on Fraud control, Triage, TPA management, and Cost containment. [Linked Themes: Claims Management].
- **P4: Risk Mgmt & Reinsurance (Protect)**: Focus on Risk Taxonomy, Reinsurance structures, and SCR/Capital. [Linked Themes: Risks, Reinsurance, Risk Management & Capital].
- **P5: Valuation & Data (Measure)**: Focus on BEL/Risk Margin, IBNR methods, Data quality, and Investments. [Linked Themes: Reserving & Valuation, Data, Models, Investments].
- **P6: Regulation & Ethics (Comply)**: Focus on IRDAI 2024 updates, PPH, and the Appointed Actuary role. [Linked Themes: Regulation, IRDAI Regulations, Professionalism].
Pricing: - **Data & Assumptions**: Experience vs Industry; internal/external credibility; proxies for new products.
- **Demographic Assumptions**: Morbidity/Mortality trends; initial vs selective selection; anti-selection risk.
- **Financial Assumptions**: Risk-free yield curves; medical inflation (~12-15% India); expense loadings (initial/renewal).
- **Risk Profile**: Impact of age, gender, occupation, and geography on claim frequency and severity.
- **Methodology**: Equation of Value (simplistic) vs. Cashflow Projection (dynamic); stochastic vs deterministic.
- **Profitability & Capital**: Target Return on Capital (RoC); Cost of Capital; risk-based capital allocation (SCR).
- **Commercial Constraints**: Competitor benchmarking; sales channel commissions; affordability vs adequacy.
- **Cross-subsidy**: Inter-generational (young subsidizing old) vs Intra-group; regulatory limits on subsidies.
- **Sensitivity & Options**: Stress testing for lapses, medical advances, and option-take up (e.g. renewal guarantees).
Claims Management: - **Notification & Proof**: Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions.
- **Assessment / Triage**: Non-disclosure checks, tele-interviews, use of specialized medical officers.
- **Control & Mitigation**: Home visits (IP), second opinions, independent medical exams.
- **Rehabilitation (IP)**: Early intervention, proportionate benefits for partial return, funding treatment to reduce claim duration.
- **Payment / Settlement**: Annuity vs lump sum, tax implications, currency, avoiding over-insurance.
Regulation: - **Key 2024 Updates**: 60-month moratorium (not 48); PED waiting period max 36 months; 1-hr/3-hr cashless TATs; 30-day Free Look; no entry age limits; Board-governed commission via PMC.
- **Key 2024 Updates**: 48-month moratorium; 1-hr/3-hr cashless timelines; 30-day Free Look; removal of entry age limits; Board-governed commission.
- **Prudential Norms**: Available Solvency Margin (ASM) >= 150% of Required Solvency Margin (RSM); statutory reserves certified by AA.
- **State Interaction**: Integration with PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat); public provision defining the floor for private demand.
- **Conduct of Business**: Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) principles; mandatory 15-day grievance resolution via GRM.
- **Capital Frameworks**: Solvency II Pillar 1/2/3 analogues; transition to Risk-Based Capital (RBC) and IFRS 17/Ind AS 117.
- **Taxation**: Section 80D benefits (up to Rs 1L for seniors); 18% GST impact on premiums and affordability.
- **Grievance Redressal**: Ombudsman hierarchy (internal -> Ombudsman -> Consumer Forum -> Civil Court).
- **Professional Roles**: Statutory role of Appointed Actuary (AA); whistleblowing; compliance with IAI APS 33/34.
IRDA Regulations: - **Health Insurance Regulations 2024**: Consolidates and replaces 2016 rules; in force 1 April 2024; PED defined as condition diagnosed/treated within 36 months prior to policy commencement; PED waiting period capped at max 36 months continuous coverage; specific disease waiting period also max 36 months; moratorium = 60 continuous months including portability and migration credits — after which no policy or claim contestable except on grounds of fraud; enhanced sum insured carries fresh 60-month clock; insurer cannot deny renewal on grounds of prior claims; no fresh medical at renewal unless sum insured being increased; grace period 15 days (monthly) / 30 days (other modes); for indemnity policies policyholder chooses primary insurer who coordinates with others for balance amount; for benefit policies claims may be made from all insurers simultaneously; NCB may be offered as cumulative bonus (increase in SI without premium increase) or discount on renewal premium — policyholder chooses form at renewal.

- **Insurance Products Regulations 2024**: Repeals IRDAI (Health Insurance) Regulations 2016, IRDAI (ULIP) Regulations 2019, IRDAI (Non-Linked) Regulations 2019 and others; life insurers may offer benefit basis only (not indemnity); general/health insurers may offer both indemnity and benefit basis; insurer must offer products catering to all ages and all medical conditions including PED, chronic conditions, disabilities, HIV/AIDS, mental illness, and all systems of medicine; AYUSH must be covered at par with allopathy; Board-approved AYUSH policy required; policyholder to have choice of treatment system; premium must remain unchanged during policy term; renewal loadings applied at portfolio level not individual policy level; discounts permitted for good claims experience; File and Use — prior filing to IRDAI and UIN assignment required before launch; Use and File — insurer may launch after UIN assignment without prior IRDAI filing but PMC approval still required.

- **Protection of Policyholders' Interests 2024**: Insurer must decide on proposal form within 7 days of receipt; policy document to be issued within 15 days of acceptance; Free Look period = 30 days from receipt of policy document for policies with term >= 1 year; proportionate premium refund if cancelled during Free Look with no claim; refund processed within 7 days of Free Look cancellation request; delay in refund attracts interest at bank rate + 2% from date of request; mandatory Customer Information Sheet (CIS) with every policy covering sum insured, coverage, exclusions, sub-limits, deductibles, waiting periods, Free Look, portability, moratorium; CIS available in local language on request; no claim repudiated without PMC or 3-member Claims Review Committee (CRC) approval; reason for repudiation must cite specific policy terms; documents to be collected by insurer/TPA — policyholder not required to submit; cashless authorisation within 1 hour; final discharge authorisation within 3 hours; delay beyond 3 hours — additional hospital charges borne by insurer from shareholders' funds; claims not settled within timelines attract interest at bank rate + 2% paid suo-moto by insurer; grievance hierarchy: internal GRM → Insurance Ombudsman → Consumer Forum → Civil Court; Ombudsman award must be complied with within 30 days; non-compliance attracts Rs 5,000 per day penalty to complainant plus penal interest; separate servicing channel required for senior citizen health insurance claims and grievances.

- **Expenses of Management (EOM) 2024**: EOM limit for standalone health insurers = 35% of gross premium written in India per financial year; EOM limit for general insurers = 30% of gross premium written; removal of individual product commission caps; commission and all expenses governed by Board-approved EOM policy reviewed annually; Board policy on commission must specify structure, fairness and reasonableness criteria, good distribution practice principles, conflict of interest safeguards, and compliance monitoring framework; commission must be commensurate with effort to acquire and service business; intermediaries must not recommend products solely to earn higher commission; new commission structures not applicable to in-force policies; IRDAI may exercise forbearance in case of excess EOM subject to conditions; insurer must file Return of EOM in prescribed formats certified by Appointed Actuary.

- **Actuarial, Finance & Investment 2024**: AA eligibility for health insurer: Fellow of IAI; at least 9 years experience in general or health insurance of which at least 4 years post-fellowship (reduced by 2 years if SA3/health specialist subject passed); at least 2 of those post-fellowship years in statutory valuation or product pricing of an Indian general/health insurer; at least 3 years in middle or senior management; full-time employee of insurer; not AA of any other insurer; not over age 70; holds Certificate of Practice from IAI; no business without AA permitted; AA reports directly to CEO; AA must not simultaneously be Chief Risk Officer; health insurer must have at least one additional actuary beyond AA for pricing and valuation; AA duties include: certifying claims reserves (IBNR, IBNER, PDR) using actuarial principles; PDR maintained at insurer level but monitored at segment level; ensuring premium rates are fair; ensuring adequacy of reinsurance; identifying solvency risks and reporting to Board; informing Competent Authority if insurer fails to rectify solvency; certifying assets and liabilities under s.64V; control level of solvency margin = ASM >= 150% RSM at all times; RSM subject to minimum of 50% of minimum capital under s.6 of Insurance Act; negative IBNR for any year of occurrence treated as zero; mathematical reserves cannot fall below surrender value; all policies on bonus distribution, ALM, investment, and risk management must be Board-approved; transition to Risk-Based Capital (RBC) and IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117 framework underway.

- **Re-insurance Regulations 2018 (as amended 2023)**: Objectives: maximise retention within India with risk diversification; develop technical capability and financial capacity; secure best reinsurance coverage at reasonable cost; simplify administration. Retention — Life Insurers: minimum 25% of sum at risk under pure protection portfolios; minimum 50% under all other life portfolios; existing pre-regulation portfolios continue under existing treaties. Retention — Indian Re-insurers and FRBs: minimum 50% of Indian reinsurance business underwritten; retrocession to an IIO up to 20% of Indian business counts toward this 50% minimum. Programme filing timelines: proposed (summary format) programme submitted at least 45 days before financial year start; Board-approved final programme filed within 45 days of financial year commencement, highlighting net retention improvements and variations from prior year; CEO certification that all treaties are received, stamped, and signed (or digitally signed) submitted within 90 days of financial year start; any mid-year revision filed with Board approval within 15 days. CBR eligibility criteria (all must be met): authorised by home-country regulator for re-insurance for immediately preceding 3 continuous years; minimum credit rating BBB from Standard & Poor's or equivalent international agency for same period; home country has signed DTAA with India; meets home-country minimum solvency/capital adequacy for same period; satisfactory past claims settlement experience; placements with any international pool or risk-sharing arrangement with CBR members also require prior Authority approval. Order of placement preference (cedants transacting non-life): Category 1 — Indian Re-insurers; Category 2 — FRBs and IIOs investing 100% of retained Indian premiums in DTA; Category 3 — other IIOs; Category 4 — other Indian Insurers (facultative, per-risk only in registered segment) and CBRs; cedant may not offer participation to a reinsurer or IIO that is a group/associate company of another Indian Insurer; an FRB/IIO that declined or did not quote may be skipped. Cession limits to individual CBRs (non-life, % of total premium ceded outside India): rated above A+ — maximum 20%; rated BBB+ up to A+ — maximum 15%; rated BBB or BBB+ — maximum 10%; no placement beyond these limits without prior Authority approval; small cedants placing total reinsurance premiums outside India up to ₹75 crore in a financial year are exempt from per-CBR cession limits provided CBR is rated BBB+ and above. Life insurer special rule: no arrangements with promoter/associate/group company without prior Authority approval unless on commercially competitive, arm's-length terms; life insurers must endeavour to utilise domestic capacity before offering to CBRs.

- **Registration, Capital Structure, Transfer of Shares and Amalgamation Regulations 2024**: Minimum paid-up equity capital: Life insurance — ₹100 crore; General insurance — ₹100 crore; Health insurance (exclusive) — ₹100 crore; Reinsurance (exclusive) — ₹200 crore; equity shares issued at face value until commencement of business; premium issuance permitted only after commencement. Registration process (sequential stages): NOC — filed online with IRDAI, valid 6 months (extendable by 3 months); applicant name must contain 'insurance,' 'assurance,' or 'reinsurance'; R1 approval — application with incorporation certificate, MoA/AoA, promoter annual reports (up to 5 years), shareholders' agreement, 5-year business projections certified by Fellow Actuary of IAI, ₹5 lakh non-refundable fee; R1 valid 3 months (extendable by 3 months); R2 approval — affidavits on paid-up capital adequacy, foreign investment compliance (74% ceiling for companies; 26% ceiling for co-operative societies), CA/CS certificate of statutory compliance, ₹5 lakh fee; R3 (Certificate of Registration) — insurer must commence business within 12 months (extendable to maximum 24 months); promoters/investors must remain fit and proper on continuous basis. Lock-in periods (unlisted insurers): investment at or before R3 — 5 years from R3 for both promoters and investors; investment during years 1–5 post-R3 — earlier of 5 years from investment date or 8 years from R3; investment during years 5–10 — promoter: earlier of 3 years from investment or 12 years from R3; investor: earlier of 2 years from investment or 11 years from R3; investment during years 10–15 — promoter: 2 years from investment; investor: 1 year; investment after 15 years — promoter: 1 year; investor: nil; lock-in does not apply to shares of listed insurers, ESOP/director scheme shares, or investor holding ≤1% of equity. Promoter holding requirements: collective promoter stake must remain above 50%; may dilute to minimum 26% only if insurer has maintained solvency above control level for 5 preceding years and shares are listed on Indian stock exchange. Investor limits: single investor — less than 25% of paid-up equity; all investors collectively — less than 50% (restriction inapplicable if listed); investor holding ≤10% — may invest in any number of insurers but no director nomination right; investor holding 10–25% — restricted to not more than 2 insurers per class, may nominate maximum 1 director; no shareholder may nominate a director in an insurer of the same class if it has already nominated a director in another insurer of that class. Transfer of shares (prior approval required): transferee's holding likely to exceed 5% of paid-up equity or any subsequent transfer taking holding past further 5% increments in a financial year; nominal value transferred by individual/firm/group/body corporate exceeding 1% of paid-up equity in a financial year; application fee ₹5 lakh (₹50 lakh if transfer exceeds 50% of paid-up equity); for listed insurers: transfers of 1–5% by non-promoters require only self-certification; acquisitions taking holding above 5% require prior approval; those taking holding above 10% require fresh prior approval. Other Forms of Capital (Preference Shares and Subordinated Debt): must be fully paid-up in cash, unsecured, non-convertible, no put option; minimum maturity — 10 years for life/general/reinsurers, 7 years for health insurers; call option may be exercised only after 5 completed years; prior Authority approval needed to exercise call option if post-exercise solvency falls below 120% of control level (i.e., below 1.8x); aggregate OFC limit — lower of 50% of paid-up equity plus securities premium or 50% of net worth; solvency haircut (straight-line over final 5 years): 5+ years to maturity — 100% counted; 4–5 years — 80%; 3–4 years — 60%; 2–3 years — 40%; 1–2 years — 20%; less than 1 year — 0%; haircut applied quarterly. Annual fee: higher of ₹10 lakh or 1/20th of 1% of gross direct premium in preceding year, capped at ₹15 crore; due by 31 January of preceding financial year; late payment penalty 2% (within 30 days of due date) or 10% (before financial year end); non-payment by financial year-end renders CoR liable for cancellation.

- **Appointed Actuary Regulations 2022**: Eligibility: Fellow of IAI; full-time employee; holds Certificate of Practice; not AA of any other insurer; not over 70; no professional misconduct; IRDAI approval required; AA has statutory right to attend and speak at Board, management, shareholder, and policyholder meetings on matters affecting solvency or policyholder interests; no letter of appointment may restrict AA's statutory duties; AA must inform Competent Authority within 7 days of any disciplinary proceedings initiated against him/her; insurer must not obstruct AA in performing duties; AA may not simultaneously be CRO; AA must comply with IAI APS 33/34 and other professional standards; dual reporting obligation to Board and IRDAI.
Risks: ### SP1 Textbook: Nature of Risks (Chapters 23-24)
- **Nature of Risk Components**: Model Risk (inappropriate structure); Parameter Risk (wrong variables used); Random Fluctuations (sample error/heterogeneity).
- **Policy & Other Data Risks**: Inaccurate/incomplete records; Model Point risk (not representing portfolio); External data mismatch (e.g., using USA data for India).
- **Claim Rates (Morbidity/Mortality)**: Future parameters uncertain due to new diseases/trends; 'Expanding Funnel of Doubt' as projections go further.
- **Claim Amounts (PMI specific)**: Short-term variability in procedure costs; medical advances impact (e.g. hip replacement rehab time shifts).
- **Earlier Screening & Diagnosis**: Critical Illness risk—earlier diagnosis = earlier payment; screening prompts claims that might have otherwise lapsed.
- **Investment Performance & Assets**: Capital values risk (snapshot solvency); low reserves in CI/PMI mean low investment importance vs long-term products.
- **Expenses and Inflation**: Mismatch between inflation and loadings; volume impact (low volume = high fixed costs per policy); third-party TPA fees.
- **Persistency & Selective Withdrawal**: Selective effect (healthy leave, substandard remain); amortization risk (initial costs not recouped if early lapse).
- **Mix & Volume of New Business**: Mix by nature/size (smaller policies = lower contribution to fixed costs); Volume risk (High = capital drain; Low = overhead mismatch).
- **Guarantees and Options**: Complexity in terms offered in advance; health events harder to predict than life events; requirement for stochastic models.
- **Competition & Management Actions**: Reduced price/increased coverage to stay competitive; Management may override Actuary's prudence for market share.
- **Counterparties (RI/Distributors/TPAs)**: Reinsurer default; Distributor holding premiums; TPA commitment of expenditure on behalf of insurer.
- **Legal, Regulatory & Tax**: Retrospective changes overriding policy terms; prohibition of certain rating factors (e.g., race/genetics).
- **Reputation & Operational Fraud**: Disrepute via media/social media (especially for sick/elderly); Internal fraud / Audit failure (Embezzlement).
- **Aggregation, Concentration & Cat**: Localized outbreaks (Aggregation); industry concentration (e.g. mining industrial disease); Pandemic/War/Terrorism.
- **Non-disclosure & Anti-selection**: Moratorium approach complications; asymmetric info at proposal; genetic testing debate.
- **Product-specific: IP Insurance**: Sickness transition probabilities (inception/termination); moral hazard (return to work disincentive); leniency of definition.
- **Product-specific: CI Insurance**: Diagnosis rates; limited historical data in new markets; rider vs standalone capital requirements.
- **Product-specific: LTC Insurance**: High claim inception; transfer between dependency levels; investment/reserve significance; marketing risk (expectation of 100% cover).
- **Product-specific: PMI**: Third-party control over claims (consultants/doctors); single large claims vs accumulation; low capital strain vs long-term.
Underwriting: - **Objective**: Prevent anti-selection, classify risks equitably, maintain competitor parity.
- **Alternative Approaches**: Moratorium underwriting (often PMI), Free Cover Limits (Group).
- **Outcomes**: Standard rates, rated premiums (+%), exclusions, deferral, decline.
- **Operational**: Cost vs. Value of info, speed of onboarding (straight-through processing).
Risk Management & Capital: - **Quantification**: Stress and Scenario testing, Internal Models vs. Standard Formula.
- **Mitigation**: Reinsurance, Asset-Liability Matching (ALM), product redesign (reviewable premiums), stricter claims control.
- **Capital**: Economic capital vs. Regulatory capital, risk appetite statements, diversification benefits.
Reserving & Valuation: - **Components**: Mathematical reserves (BEL), Risk Margin, Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR), Outstanding Claims (OSLR), IBNR.
- **Methods**: Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Chain Ladder / Run-off triangles (short-tailed), Cashflow projection (long-tailed).
- **Differences**: Pricing (realistic + profit margin) vs. Supervisory (prudent/margins) vs. Embedded Value (market consistent).
- **Prudence**: Allowance for adverse deviation, discounting at risk-free rates.
Reinsurance: - **Financial**: Capital relief, profit smoothing, increase capacity.
- **Risk**: Transfer large single risks (Surplus), catastrophic events (Cat XL), aggregate shocks (Stop Loss).
- **Strategic**: 'Badging' / white-labeling, new market entry, technical underwriting assistance.
- **Types**: Proportional (Quota Share / Surplus) vs. Non-proportional (Excess of Loss).
Product Design: - **Target Market**: Segmenting by age, income, and needs; filling gaps in State provision (PMJAY etc).
- **Benefits & Conditions**: Core coverage; waiting/deferred periods; benefit limits; reinstatement terms.
- **Exclusions**: Standard medical exclusions; occupational/lifestyle risks; territorial limits.
- **Options & Guarantees**: GIOs (no medicals); conversion options; guaranteed renewability/premiums.
- **Surrender Values**: Discontinuance benefits (usually nil for indemnity PMI/CI); impact on model pricing.
- **Marketability**: Brand positioning; 'saleability' vs 'adequacy'; bundling CI/IP riders onto life cover.
- **Constraints**: IT/Admin platform capacity; reinsurance treaties; commission-driven distribution.
- **Risks**: Moral hazard (over-insurance); anti-selection at entry; complexity leading to mis-selling.
- **Profitability/Capital**: Solvency II capital strain (Pillar 1); hurdle rates; ROI vs VNB targets.
Data: - **Data Issues**: Lack of volume, heterogeneity, changes in internal processes, coding errors, lags in reporting.
- **Solutions**: Reinsurer data, Population proxies (adjusted), Grouping data.
- **Data Quality**: Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness (TAS 100 / AS 34).
Models: - **Model Choice**: Multi-state (Markov) vs. Formula/Equation of Value. Deterministic vs Stochastic.
- **Governance**: Peer review, backtesting against actual experience, sensitivity testing.
- **Parameters**: Best estimate vs. Prudent, allowance for medical inflation and longevity.
Group Products: - **Structure**: Master policyholder (Employer), simplified admin, cheaper unit costs.
- **Underwriting**: Free Cover Limits (FCL), 'actively at work' condition, minimal medical info.
- **Risks**: Concentration risk (one building explodes), anti-selection if voluntary take-up, changing workforce demographics.
- **Pricing**: Experience rating (past claims) vs. Community rating, 1 or 3-year rate guarantees.
Investments: - **Nature of Liabilities**: Currency, term/duration, fixed vs real (inflation-linked).
- **Matching**: Match duration to immunize against yield curve shifts. Real assets (Equities/Property) for inflation, fixed-interest for non-linked.
- **Constraints**: Regulatory limitations on asset classes, Liquidity needs (PMI claims are sudden), Solvency II capital charges on risky assets.
Professional Guidance: - **Standards**: Following APS/TAS (e.g. TAS 100), ensuring advice is clear, comprehensive, and unbiased.
- **Role**: Statutory duties (Chief Actuary / With-Profits Actuary), Peer review requirements.
- **Ethics**: Whistleblowing, managing conflicts of interest, handling data quality issues responsibly.
Capital & Solvency: - **Role of Capital**: Provide confidence to policyholders and regulators, buffer against shocks.
- **Solvency II**: Pillar 1 (Quantitative: BEL, Risk Margin, SCR), Pillar 2 (Qualitative: ORSA, Governance), Pillar 3 (Disclosure).
- **Management**: Optimizing capital via Reinsurance or ALM, Return on Capital (ROC) thresholds.
Private Medical Insurance (PMI): - **Nature**: Short-term (annual renewable), high frequency/low severity claims.
- **Pricing**: Community rating, Age-banding, Medical inflation is a massive driver (super-inflation).
- **Claims**: Pre-authorization, Provider networks, Excesses/Co-payments to reduce trivial claims.
- **Underwriting**: Moratorium is very common, FMU (Full Medical) for comprehensive cover.
Critical Illness: - **Nature**: Long-term, fixed lump-sum payment upon diagnosis of defined condition.
- **Risks**: Changes in medical science (earlier diagnosis), anti-selection, definition disputes.
- **Features**: Survival period (usually 14-28 days), standalone vs accelerated (attached to life cover), severity-based payouts.
Income Protection: - **Nature**: Replacement of lost earnings due to incapacity. Regular payments.
- **Key Levers**: Deferred period (DP) length, Replacement Ratio (max 50-75% to maintain incentive to work).
- **Definition**: Own occupation vs Any occupation vs Activities of Daily Work.
- **Risks**: Moral hazard, economic downturns heavily increase claim rates ('unemployment disguise').
Pricing & Rating: - **Age/Gender**: Classic variables (though gender restricted in EU).
- **Experience Rating**: Adjusting renewal premiums based on past group claims.
- **NCD**: No Claims Discounts (common in PMI) to prevent trivial claims but hurts retention if lost.
- **Reviewability**: Guaranteed (fixed) vs Reviewable (can hike rates if experience worsens over the block).
State Healthcare & Demographics: - **State Role**: Universal coverage (PMJAY / NHS), means-tested social care.
- **Private Role**: Que-jumping, luxury facilities, covering non-critical procedures.
- **Demographics**: Aging populations increase LTC and medical costs, shrinking taxpayer bases.
- **Healthcare Economics**: QALYs (Quality Adjusted Life Years) to decide drug funding via agencies like NICE.
Long-Term Care (LTC): - **Nature**: Payout triggered by failure of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) or cognitive impairment.
- **Types**: Pre-funded (regular premiums while healthy) vs Immediate Needs Annuity (lump sum at point of care).
- **Risks**: Extreme longevity risk, massive care-cost inflation, reputational risk if claims denied in old age.
Legislation & Taxation: - **Premiums/Benefits**: Are premiums tax deductible? Are benefits tax free? Drives product demand.
- **Employer Tax**: Incentivizes Group PMI / Group IP if treated favorably as business expense.
- **Anti-Discrimination**: Equality acts preventing pricing based on genetics, gender, or disabilities without pure statistical proof.
Product Design (Protection): - **Standalone vs Riders**: Selling CI or Death cover attached to a savings/mortgage plan.
- **Unit-Linked**: Charging mortality/morbidity costs by deducting units from a fund (reviewable by nature).
- **Options**: Conversion options, Guaranteed Insurability Options (GIOs) without medicals.
Administration: - **Setup Constraints**: Can legacy IT handle complex tiered benefits or unit-deductions?
- **Customer Journey**: Quotation portals, premium collection methods (Direct Debit).
- **Outsourcing**: Using Third Party Administrators (TPAs) for claims, call centers. Retaining oversight.
